title: Activated-charcoal cold-process soap
subtitle: A jet-black cold-process bar with activated charcoal and tea-tree essential oil
excerpt: A cold-process bar built on coconut, olive, and castor oils with food-grade activated charcoal mixed into the lye-water for a deep black bar. Tea-tree essential oil sits on top of the colour. Suits oily skin and grimy hands. Allow 6 weeks to harden before use.
sourceNotes: Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997): coconut/olive/castor ratios. Activated charcoal at 1-2 tsp per 500 g oils is the standard cold-process dosing across the soapmaking literature. Tea-tree at 1.5% of oils for a leave-on bar follows Tisserand and Young, Essential Oil Safety (2014).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg bar soap (~7 x 130 g bars after cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 2 kg cleanly. Beyond that, batch in 1 kg lots.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The bar keeps a year or more in a cool dark cupboard. Longer cures give a harder, milder bar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Stable 2 years cured and stored cool. The black bar transfers slightly when wet so use a draining soap dish or the bar will stain pale ceramic. Discard if orange spots appear (DOS, rancid superfat) or the smell turns sharp.

glossaryTerms.definition: Food-grade activated charcoal (from coconut shell or bamboo) at 1-2 tsp per 500 g oils gives a jet-black bar. It's a passive colourant; the porous surface area binds some oil and grime during use, which is why charcoal bars suit oily skin and grimy hands. Use food or cosmetic grade only - industrial charcoal can contain heavy-metal contamination.

content.text: A jet-black bar built on the same coconut/olive/castor base as a basic body bar, with food-grade activated charcoal dispersed into the lye-water for a uniform deep colour. Tea-tree essential oil at 1.5% of oils sits on top. The bar suits oily skin, grimy mechanic hands, and post-garden cleanup. It will transfer a faint grey on a white flannel; use a coloured washcloth.
recipe at 6% superfat and 33% lye discount. Cure 6 weeks. The
goes into the cooled lye solution so it disperses without clumping; folding it in at trace gives streaks.

: safety goggles, nitrile gloves, long sleeves, closed shoes. Ventilate the kitchen. Pour lye into water, never water into lye. Keep cold running water available to flush a splash. Tea-tree essential oil at 1.5% is below the dermal limit for leave-on products but skip on the face if you have sensitive skin.

items.prepNote: Pomace or refined olive oil. Extra-virgin works but gives a slightly softer bar.
Refined coconut. Solid at room temperature; melt over a water bath.
Adds creamy bubbles. Cap at 10% of oils.
Refined shea. Melt with the coconut. Adds conditioning and bar hardness.
Lye, 6% superfat calculation. Weigh to 1 g precision.
Food-grade activated charcoal, from coconut shell or bamboo. About 2 tsp. Stir into the cooled lye solution; will turn the solution opaque black.

content.text: Goggles and gloves on. Weigh the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Weigh the sodium hydroxide separately. Take the pitcher outdoors or to the extractor. Add the lye to the water a tablespoon at a time, stirring with a stainless or silicone spatula until dissolved. The solution heats to 80-90 degrees Celsius. Set aside to cool. When the lye solution drops to 50 degrees Celsius, stir in the activated charcoal until uniform black. Continue cooling to 38-42 degrees Celsius.

Weigh the coconut oil and shea butter into a stainless or heatproof glass bowl. Melt over a low water bath until liquid. Add the olive and castor oils. Stir. Aim to match the lye temperature within 5 degrees Celsius - both around 38-42 degrees Celsius.

Pour the black lye-charcoal solution into the oils through a fine strainer. Pulse the stick blender in 5-second bursts, stirring between, until the batter reaches trace - 3-5 minutes. At light trace (custard consistency, leaves a faint trail), add the tea-tree essential oil. Stir by hand to disperse.

Pour the batter into the silicone loaf mould. Tap to release air bubbles. Cover and rest 24-48 hours undisturbed. Unmould when firm; cut into 7 x 130 g bars. Stand on a wire rack in a cool airy spot, turn every few days for the first week, cure 6 weeks before use. The bar darkens to a deep matte black over the cure.

Store unwrapped in a wooden soap box or wrapped in waxed paper. 2 years cool and dry. Use a draining dish; the charcoal will stain a pale ceramic dish if the bar sits in puddled water. Discard if orange spots develop or the smell goes sharp.
